channel.title: NYT > World News
channel.copyright: Copyright 2025 The New York Times Company

item.title: Israeli Strikes on Gaza Kills Over 400, in Breakdown of Cease-Fire
item.description: Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu’s office said he had ordered the aerial attacks after Hamas’s “repeated refusal” to release the remaining hostages it holds. “This is just the beginning,” he said.
item.dc:creator: Patrick Kingsley, Rawan Sheikh Ahmad, Yan Zhuang, Aaron Boxerman and Michael Levenson
item.media:credit: Saher Alghorra for The New York Times
item.media:description: Palestinians at a hospital in Gaza City on Tuesday with the body of someone killed in Israeli airstrikes.

item.title: Putin Agrees to Limited Cease-Fire on Ukraine Energy Targets
item.description: In a call with President Trump, Russia’s leader agreed to pause strikes on energy infrastructure. Ukraine also appeared willing to accept such a halt, though it fell short of the unconditional cease-fire the country had already agreed to.
item.dc:creator: David E. Sanger and Paul Sonne
item.media:description: President Vladimir V. Putin of Russia held a call with President Trump on Tuesday.

item.title: Pelicot’s Daughter Pursues Conviction That He Raped Her, Too
item.description: Her mother, Gisèle, was at the center of a trial that gripped France and made her a feminist icon. But it left Caroline Darian with her own pains and suspicions unanswered.
item.dc:creator: Catherine Porter
item.media:credit: Andrea Mantovani for The New York Times
item.media:description: “My case, in that court, it was like it didn’t exist,” said the daughter of Gisèle and Dominique Pelicot. She uses the pen name Caroline Darian.

item.title: Americans Describe Being Detained as Hostages in a Venezuelan Prison
item.description: They were American tourists hoping for a good time, they said. Then they became captives of an autocratic government.
item.dc:creator: Julie Turkewitz
item.media:credit: Caitlin O'Hara for The New York Times
item.media:description: Gregory David Werber, who had been detained in Venezuela, in Tempe, Ariz., on Thursday.

item.title: Trump and Putin Discuss Ukraine-Russia Cease-Fire: What to Know
item.description: The Kremlin said that President Vladimir V. Putin had agreed in a call with President Trump to temporarily stop strikes on Ukrainian energy infrastructure.
item.dc:creator: Constant Méheut and Ivan Nechepurenko
item.media:credit: Doug Mills/The New York Times; Kristina Kormilitsyna/Sputnik

item.title: Drones Will Do Some Schlepping for Sherpas on Mount Everest
item.description: The devices will be used to airlift heavy, dangerous loads normally carried by mountain guides. They might even deliver a hot meal or two.
item.dc:creator: Bhadra Sharma and Mujib Mashal
item.media:description: A Sherpa in Nepal’s Everest region, with Mount Kongde in the background. More guides have died in accidents on Mount Everest as snowmelt has accelerated.

item.title: Canada Announces Arctic Radar Deal With Australia Amid Trump Threats
item.description: As President Trump talks about U.S. dominance of the Arctic, Canada says it will spend billions to defend the region, including buying new radar technology from Australia.
item.dc:creator: Matina Stevis-Gridneff
item.media:credit: Alex Welsh for The New York Times
item.media:description: The northern lights illuminating the sky above a former Distant Early Warning radar station in the Canadian Arctic, a legacy of the Cold War.

item.title: Germany Is Lifting a Foot Off Its ‘Debt Brake.’ Here’s Why.
item.description: An incoming government wants to borrow much more to revamp the economy and rebuild the military. That means a change in the country’s Constitution — and its culture.
item.dc:creator: Jim Tankersley and Christopher F. Schuetze
item.media:description: Friedrich Merz, Germany’s chancellor in waiting, is attempting to make a constitutional change even before he takes office.

item.title: Chinese Nationalists Praise Trump’s Cuts to Voice of America
item.description: Beijing has long criticized the outlet, as well as Radio Free Asia, for highlighting human rights abuses in China.
item.dc:creator: Tiffany May
item.media:description: Voice of America’s ability to operate is in question after President Trump signed an executive order calling for the dismantling of the federal agency that oversees it.

item.title: South Africa Will Not Remove Antarctic Team After a Reported Assault
item.description: A researcher at a South African base in Antarctica has been accused of physical assault and sexual harassment. South Africa said it had no immediate plans to remove the accused or any colleagues.
item.dc:creator: Zimasa Matiwane and Amelia Nierenberg
item.media:description: Antarctica’s harsh conditions have routinely made it difficult for countries to scrutinize researchers’ behavior there.

item.title: UK Aims to Cut Billions in Welfare Amid Budget Crunch
item.description: Changing disability allowances is a particularly contentious move within Prime Minister Keir Starmer’s center-left Labour Party.
item.dc:creator: Stephen Castle
item.media:description: Liz Kendall, Britain’s work and pensions secretary, told Parliament on Tuesday that the cuts aim to get those who can work into jobs, protect those who cannot work and save taxpayers money.

item.title: As France Sours on Trump’s America, De Gaulle Is on the Rise
item.description: Charles de Gaulle’s insistence on French power and autonomy is being cited widely in France; one politician demanded that an America on “the side of the tyrants” return the Statue of Liberty.
item.dc:creator: Roger Cohen
item.media:credit: Stephane De Sakutin/Agence France-Presse — Getty Images

item.title: Lucy Letby Murders Inquiry Should Be Halted, Hospital’s Ex-Managers Say
item.description: Former executives at the British hospital where Ms. Letby was a nurse requested that a judge’s inquiry into the killings there be suspended amid new evidence.
item.dc:creator: Megan Specia
item.media:description: The Countess of Chester Hospital in 2023. Lucy Letby was convicted of murdering seven babies while she was a neonatal nurse there about a decade ago.

item.title: Trump’s Focus on Ukrainian Nuclear Plant Is Partly Linked to Minerals Deal, Officials Say
item.description: The U.S. is seeking access to Ukrainian minerals, which require extensive energy to process. The Zaporizhzhia power plant in southern Ukraine, which Russia controls, could help with that, Kyiv says.
item.dc:creator: Constant Méheut
item.media:credit: Finbarr O'Reilly for The New York Times
item.media:description: The Zaporizhzhia nuclear power plant in 2022. The facility was seized by Russia early in the war.

item.title: Earth’s 10 Hottest Years Have Been the Last 10
item.description: A report from the World Meteorological Organization confirms that 2024 was the hottest year on record and the first year to be more than 1.5 degrees Celsius above the preindustrial era.
item.dc:creator: Delger Erdenesanaa
item.media:description: Pedestrians took a break on the Brooklyn Bridge during a heat wave last year.

item.title: Parents of Student Missing in the Dominican Republic Believe She Drowned
item.description: The student, Sudiksha Konanki, 20, was on a spring break trip to Punta Cana when she disappeared earlier this month, leading to a massive search effort and an investigation.
item.dc:creator: Aimee Ortiz
item.media:description: The Dominican authorities deployed drones, helicopters, divers, boats, police dogs and other resources to search around Punta Cana after Ms. Konanki was reported missing earlier this month.

item.title: Son of Jair Bolsonaro Says He Will Seek Political Asylum in the U.S.
item.description: Eduardo Bolsonaro, a son of Brazil’s former president, said he planned to stay in the United States, claiming persecution back home.
item.dc:creator: Ana Ionova
item.media:description: Eduardo Bolsonaro during a visit to the White House in 2019, during President Trump’s first term.

item.title: Wednesday Briefing: Russia Agreed to Halt Some Strikes
item.description: Plus, Ohtani’s star power in Japan.
item.dc:creator: Emmett Lindner
item.media:credit: Mauricio Lima for The New York Times
item.media:description: A Ukrainian thermal power plant damaged by Russian missile strikes last year.

item.title: John “Paddy” Hemingway, Last Surviving Pilot of the Battle of Britain, Dies at 105
item.description: A fighter pilot in a vastly outnumbered Royal Air Force — one of the “few” hailed by Churchill — he took to the skies to help stave off a Nazi land invasion of Britain.
item.dc:creator: Richard Goldstein
item.media:description: Mr. Hemingway posed in 2022 in front of a Hurricane fighter plane, the kind he flew in the Battle of Britain.

item.title: Hamas Does Not Respond Militarily to Renewed Israeli Attack on Gaza
item.description: “Hamas doesn’t want an escalation, but it will not surrender,” said one Palestinian analyst close to the militant group, which has been weakened by a 15-month war with Israel.
item.dc:creator: Adam Rasgon
item.media:credit: Saher Alghorra for The New York Times
item.media:description: Hamas fighters during a handover of Israeli hostages last month.

item.title: Why Did Israel Resume Airstrikes on Gaza? What to Know About the Attacks
item.description: The deadly airstrikes shattered a period of relative calm and raised the prospect of a return to all-out war.
item.dc:creator: Matthew Mpoke Bigg and Ephrat Livni
item.media:credit: Saher Alghorra for The New York Times
item.media:description: Praying over the bodies of Palestinians killed in Gaza City on Tuesday.

item.title: Russia Escalated Sabotage to Pressure U.S. and Allies on Ukraine, Study Says
item.description: Anger at Russia’s covert efforts, which included targeting undersea cables, warehouses and railways, has the potential to influence European reactions to the U.S.-led push for an end to the war.
item.dc:creator: Julian E. Barnes
item.category: Center for Strategic and International Studies
item.media:description: Poland blamed Russia for a fire at a shopping center in Warsaw last May.

item.title: In Europe and Japan, Luxury Sleeper Trains
item.description: From the Lake District in England to a lush island in Japan, here’s a way to travel — and sleep — in style.
item.dc:creator: Stephanie Rosenbloom

item.title: U.S. Foreign Aid Cuts Hit Sudanese Refugees Fleeing Violence and Famine in Darfur
item.description: Just weeks after the U.S. government suspended its work in massive foreign aid cuts announced in January, humanitarians say much of the damage to critical programs has already been done.
item.dc:creator: Alex Pena
item.media:credit: Daniel Vergara for The New York Times

item.title: Small Plane Crashes Off Roatán Island in Honduras, Killing 12
item.description: The police said five people had survived the crash off Roatán, an island tourist destination. Among the dead was Aurelio Martínez, a popular Honduran musician and politician.
item.dc:creator: Jonathan Wolfe and Mike Ives
item.media:credit: Honduran Air Force, via Agence France-Presse — Getty Images
item.media:description: A photograph supplied by the Honduran Air Force showing emergency workers caring for an injured person after the crash off Roatán.

item.title: Israel Resumes Attacks in Gaza After Stalled Cease-Fire Talks with Hamas
item.description: While the Israeli government has stopped short of a ground invasion for now, its new strikes in Gaza seem to be an effort to force concessions from Hamas.
item.dc:creator: Patrick Kingsley and Ronen Bergman
item.media:description: An injured man searching through the rubble of his home in Nuseirat, in central Gaza, after an Israeli strike on Tuesday.

item.title: Tuesday Briefing
item.description: The Trump administration clashes with a judge.
item.dc:creator: Natasha Frost
item.media:description: President Trump’s administration has come close to open defiance of the federal judiciary.

item.title: Fewer Than Half of the 59 Hostages in Gaza Believed to Be Alive
item.description: Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu said Tuesday’s attack on Gaza came after Hamas said no to the latest proposals by mediators, which included releasing the remaining captives.
item.dc:creator: Jin Yu Young
item.media:description: Einav Zangauker, center, the mother of Matan Zangauker, an Israeli hostage held in Gaza, near the Gaza border in Kibbutz Nir Oz, Israel, on Tuesday.

item.title: Timeline of Trump’s Deportation Flights, From Alien Enemies Act to Judge’s Order
item.description: A New York Times review of flight data showed that at the time of a federal judge’s order, two flights were in the air, and one had not yet taken off.
item.dc:creator: Luke Broadwater, Albert Sun and Annie Correal
item.media:credit: Secretaría De Prensa De La Presidencia
item.media:description: A photograph released by El Salvador’s government showed U.S. deportees arriving at the Terrorism Confinement Center in Tecoluca, El Salvador.

item.title: Nayib Bukele, El Salvador’s Leader, Sees Opportunity in Trump’s Deportations
item.description: President Nayib Bukele’s role in the Trump administration’s deportation strategy signals a new level of power and global visibility for the young leader.
item.dc:creator: Annie Correal
item.media:credit: Office of the President of El Salvador
item.media:description: The government of El Salvador released photos on Sunday that it said showed deportees from the United States inside the Terrorism Confinement Center in Tecoluca, El Salvador.

item.title: Canada Turns to European Allies Amid Trump Threats
item.description: Days after becoming prime minister, Mark Carney visited Paris and London to highlight deep European friendships as his country fights back against President Trump’s tariffs and annexation talk.
item.dc:creator: Matina Stevis-Gridneff
item.media:description: Prime Minister Mark Carney of Canada, left, meeting on Monday with Prime Minister Keir Starmer of Britain at 10 Downing Street.

item.title: Trump Discussion With Putin to Focus on What Ukraine Will Lose
item.description: In an echo of the Yalta Conference in 1945, the American and Russian leaders will talk on Tuesday about who gets what in the process of ending the war in Ukraine.
item.dc:creator: David E. Sanger
item.media:description: The Yalta Conference of 1945, where Prime Minister Winston Churchill of Britain, President Franklin D. Roosevelt and the Soviet leader Joseph Stalin carved Europe into the American-aligned West and the Soviet-controlled East.

item.title: Tuesday Briefing: A Trump-Putin Call
item.description: Plus, a “retirement home” for penguins.
item.dc:creator: Emmett Lindner
item.media:credit: Tierney L. Cross for The New York Times
item.media:description: “We want to see if we can bring that war to an end,” President Trump said on Sunday.

item.title: Alone at Sea for 95 Days, a Peruvian Fisherman Clung to Hope
item.description: Máximo Napa Castro, 61, was spotted by the crew of an Ecuadorean tuna boat after he set out from the Peruvian port of Marcona in December.
item.dc:creator: Michael Levenson
item.media:description: Peruvian fisherman Maximo Napa spent 95 days lost in the Pacific Ocean before being rescued by an Ecuadorian fishing patrol.

item.title: Trump’s Tariff Policies Jolt Auto Industry Executives
item.description: President Trump’s approach to tariffs has unsettled many corporate leaders who believed he would use the levies as a negotiating tool. As it turns out, he sees them as an end in themselves.
item.dc:creator: Jonathan Swan, Maggie Haberman and Ana Swanson
item.media:credit: Tierney L. Cross for The New York Times
item.media:description: For corporate America, the shock of President Trump’s second term is that it turns out he really does believe that foreign countries are ripping America off and that tariffs are a silver bullet.

item.title: Émilie Dequenne, Belgian Actress Who Starred in ‘Rosetta,’ Dies at 43
item.description: She won the Cannes Film Festival’s best actress award for her debut performance in 1999. She was later diagnosed with a rare adrenal gland cancer.
item.dc:creator: Jenny Gross
item.media:credit: Patricia De Melo Moreira/Agence France-Presse — Getty Images
item.media:description: Émilie Dequenne at the 76th Cannes Film Festival in Cannes in 2023. She won best actress at Cannes when she was 17.

item.title: Trump Lawyer Refuses to Answer Judge’s Questions in Deportation Flight Hearing
item.description: The Trump administration tried to have the hearing canceled and sought to remove the judge overseeing it, as White House officials took a confrontational stance.
item.dc:creator: Alan Feuer and Zolan Kanno-Youngs
item.media:description: Judge James E. Boasberg directed a Justice Department lawyer to inform him by noon on Tuesday exactly what time the government believes his order stopping the deportation flights went into effect.

item.title: Rwanda Cuts Ties With Belgium Over Congo Conflict and E.U. Sanctions
item.description: Belgium, Rwanda’s former colonial ruler, pushed for the European Union to impose sanction against Rwandan officials over their role in invading eastern Congo and plundering its mineral wealth.
item.dc:creator: Elian Peltier
item.media:credit: Guerchom Ndebo for The New York Times
item.media:description: M23 fighters with captured Congolese troops on trucks in Goma, Democratic Republic of Congo, in January.

item.title: In Ukraine’s Frontline Town of Sloviansk, a Taste of Normalcy Beckons
item.description: Sushi has long been a popular indulgence in Ukraine. For the residents of Sloviansk, a city in Russian cross hairs, it can provide a sense of normalcy that is akin to a necessity in wartime.
item.dc:creator: Cassandra Vinograd, Dzvinka Pinchuk and Brendan Hoffman
item.media:credit: Brendan Hoffman for The New York Times

item.title: A Special St. Patrick’s Day Guest: the Head of King George V
item.description: At its show in Australia this weekend, the raucously political rap trio Kneecap brought what appeared to be the missing head of a King George V statue onstage.
item.dc:creator: Ali Watkins
item.category: George V, King of Great Britain
item.media:credit: Timothy O'Connell for The New York Times
item.media:description: The Northern Irish rap trio Kneecap brought what appeared to be the severed head of a statue of King George V onstage at a show in Melbourne, Australia.

item.title: In North Macedonia, Officials Investigate Lapses in Deadly Nightclub Fire
item.description: At least 59 people died and more than 150 were injured in the inferno. The authorities said the venue’s hospitality permit had been issued illegally.
item.dc:creator: Amelia Nierenberg and Alisa Dogramadzieva
item.media:description: Relatives of the victims of the deadly fire waiting for news at a hospital in Kocani, North Macedonia, on Sunday.

item.title: Telegram’s Pavel Durov, Charged With Range of Crimes, Is Allowed to Leave France
item.description: Pavel Durov said that the investigation into illicit activity on the messaging app he runs is ongoing, but that he had returned home to Dubai.
item.dc:creator: Eve Sampson and Adam Satariano
item.media:description: Pavel Durov, the chief executive and co-founder of the Telegram messaging app, arriving at a courthouse in Paris for a hearing in December.

item.title: Netanyahu’s Move to Fire Shin Bet Chief Reflects Wider Push for Control
item.description: The effort is part of a dispute between Benjamin Netanyahu’s right-wing alliance and its opponents about the nature and future of the Israeli state.
item.dc:creator: Patrick Kingsley
item.media:description: Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu’s effort to remove Israel’s domestic intelligence is raising concerns about whether he was seeking to undermine the agency’s independence.

item.title: Trump’s Plan to Discuss Ukraine’s Power Plants With Putin Prompts Questions
item.description: The call, scheduled for Tuesday, will be the first known conversation between the two leaders since Mr. Putin laid out numerous conditions for a cease-fire with Ukraine.
item.dc:creator: Ivan Nechepurenko and Constant Méheut
item.media:description: A photo released by Russian state media shows President Vladimir V. Putin on a video call outside of Moscow last week.

item.title: How South Sudan Returned to the Brink of War
item.description: An attack on a U.N. helicopter has highlighted rising tensions in the world’s youngest country, where a seven-year-old peace deal looks ever more fragile.
item.dc:creator: Abdi Latif Dahir
item.media:credit: Ivor Prickett for The New York Times
item.media:description: The outskirts of Juba, South Sudan’s capital, last year. The country endured several years of civil war before a fragile peace agreement in 2018.

item.title: Violence in Syria Shows Difficulty in Unifying Armed Forces
item.description: Sectarian-driven killings of civilians this month displayed the government’s weak control over both its own forces and affiliated fighters, experts said.
item.dc:creator: Ben Hubbard
item.category: Human Rights and Human Rights Violations
item.media:description: A spasm of violence erupted in Syria this month, pitting the new government’s security forces, and fighters affiliated with it, against suspected remnants of the old regime.

item.title: Want Cheap Power, Fast? Solar and Wind Firms Have a Suggestion.
item.description: Renewable energy companies are shifting strategy under President Trump, emphasizing the economic benefits of low-carbon electricity.
item.dc:creator: Brad Plumer
item.media:credit: Loren Elliott for The New York Times
item.media:description: Wind and solar companies are casting their industries as essential to achieving U.S. energy abundance, betting that argument will do better with a president who dismisses global warming.

item.title: Monday Briefing
item.description: The Trump administration deported hundreds of Venezuelans.
item.dc:creator: Natasha Frost
item.media:credit: El Salvador's Presidency Press Office, via Reuters
item.media:description: El Salvador’s government released this photo of Venezuelan deportees from the United States being taken to a prison in the municipality of Tecoluca.

item.title: Families of Jeju Air Plane Crash Victims Press for Answers
item.description: Some relatives of the 179 people killed in the worst aviation disaster in South Korea regularly return to the airport where it happened. “Only here can we cry,” one said.
item.dc:creator: John Yoon
item.media:credit: Chang W. Lee/The New York Times
item.media:description: Some relatives of those killed in the Jeju Air crash have been staying at Muan International Airport, sleeping in tents that were set up for them soon after the Dec. 29 crash.

item.title: Nary a Critical Word: Bill Gates’s Close Bond With Narendra Modi
item.description: The relationship between the billionaire philanthropist and the Indian leader helps both men meet their missions. But it also papers over the erosion of rights under Mr. Modi.
item.dc:creator: Anupreeta Das
item.media:description: Bill Gates met with Prime Minister Narendra Modi three weeks before India’s general election in 2024.

item.title: Venezuelan Families Fear for Relatives as Trump Celebrates Deportations to El Salvador
item.description: The news that hundreds of migrants were headed to an El Salvador prison caused panic for some Venezuelans, who worried that their loved ones might be among them.
item.dc:creator: Annie Correal
item.media:description: The government of Nicolás Maduro, Venezuela’s president, has denounced the United States’ deportation of 238 Venezuelans accused of gang membership to El Salvador.

item.title: Netanyahu Moves to Fire Israel’s Domestic Intelligence Chief
item.description: The Israeli prime minister’s effort to remove the Shin Bet chief is raising concerns about whether he was seeking to undermine the agency’s independence.
item.dc:creator: Adam Rasgon and Natan Odenheimer
item.media:description: Ronen Bar, the Shin Bet director, at a memorial in Jerusalem in October.

item.title: Monday Briefing: U.S. Deports Hundreds in Face of a Court Order
item.description: Plus, shadows light up Iranian history.
item.dc:creator: Emmett Lindner
item.media:description: President Nayib Bukele, left, of El Salvador hosted Secretary of State Marco Rubio last month.

item.title: How Ukraine’s Offensive in Russia’s Kursk Region Unraveled
item.description: At the height of the campaign, Ukrainian forces controlled some 500 square miles of Russian territory. Now they hold just a small sliver of land along the border.
item.dc:creator: Marc Santora
item.media:credit: Nanna Heitmann for The New York Times
item.media:description: A Russian soldier in the Kursk region in December.

item.title: Vatican Releases First Photo of Pope Francis Since He Was Hospitalized
item.description: The release of the photo a month into his treatment came after dozens of young people gathered under the pontiff’s windows to offer wishes for a quick recovery.
item.dc:creator: Elisabetta Povoledo
item.media:description: In this picture released by the Vatican Press Office, Pope Francis sits inside his chapel at the Gemelli hospital in Rome on Sunday, where he celebrated Mass with other priests.

item.title: Ukraine Retreats From Most of Russia’s Kursk Region
item.description: The bold campaign Ukraine launched last summer to seize and occupy Russian territory appears to be nearing an end.
item.dc:creator: Constant Méheut, Marc Santora and Yurii Shyvala
item.media:credit: Finbarr O'Reilly for The New York Times
item.media:description: Ukrainian military vehicles in the Sumy region near the border with Russia’s Kursk region in January.

item.title: Nightclub Fire Kills at Least 59 in North Macedonia
item.description: The tragedy occurred after fireworks set the club’s roof on fire during a pop concert, the country’s interior minister said.
item.dc:creator: Amelia Nierenberg and Alisa Dogramadzieva
